posts.raw_content:
Under the Texas Education Freedom Accounts (TEFA) program, eligibility and payouts are categorized into three distinct tiers. The key factor is how you choose to educate your child (private school vs. homeschool) and their specific learning needs.

1. Summary of Payouts (2026–27 School Year)

| Standard Private School | $10,474 | Enrolled in an accredited, approved private school. |

| Students with Disabilities | Up to $30,000 | Must have an IEP (Individualized Education Program) on file with the TEA. |

| Homeschool Students | $2,000 | Educated at home; funds are restricted to curriculum and supplies. |

2. Deep Dive: Eligibility & Requirements
A. Students with Disabilities (The $30,000 Tier)
This is the highest level of funding, designed to cover specialized therapies and services that private tuition might not include.

 * Eligibility: The child must have a disability recognized by the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA).

 * The "IEP" Rule: To get the full amount (up to $30,000), a private diagnosis is not enough. You must have a valid IEP from a Texas public school district or charter school on file.

 * Variable Amount: You don't automatically get a $30,000 check. The actual amount is calculated based on what the child’s local school district would have received to provide those specific services. Furthermore two or more specialists must confirm the condition meets the legal burden. 

B. Homeschool Students (The $2,000 Tier)
Homeschoolers receive a significantly smaller portion of the state funding because they do not have private school tuition costs.

 * Restrictions: These funds are specifically for "instructional materials," such as textbooks, online courses, and educational software.

 * The "Hybrid" Note: If a homeschool student uses a tutor or a part-time "microschool" that is an approved vendor, they may be able to access additional funds from the account for those specific services.

C. Universal Eligibility vs. Priority
While any K-12 student in Texas who is a U.S. citizen (or lawfully present) is technically eligible to apply, the state uses a "Priority Tier" system because funding is capped at $1 billion.

 * Tier 1 (Highest Priority): Students with disabilities from families earning below 500% of the Federal Poverty Level ($156,000–$160,000 for a family of four).

 * Tier 2: Low-income families (below 200% of the poverty level).

 * Tier 3: Families between 200% and 500% of the poverty level. Tier 3 targets middle class families..

 * Tier 4: Everyone else (capped at 20% of the total program budget).

3. How the Money is Handled:
**The money is not sent to your personal bank account. It is deposited into a digital account managed by a state-contracted vendor (currently Odyssey). You use that portal to pay tuition to approved schools or buy materials from approved vendors.**

Any intentional misuse of the funds is automatically a state felony. Paxton and TEA are working to ensure the money is going where intended.
